This repo is located here and deployed to a seperate repo here.

If i have made any mistakes, or you would like to contribute, please feel free to open an issue or a pull request.

docs.title: Dictionary
docs.text:
• 0fuse Console - A console with no CPU fuses burned, allowing it to boot ANY nand image for the board.

  • XeLL image - a NAND image without the CE, CF and CG; Directly from CD to XeLL in boot.

  • Shadowboot image - A NAND image designed for devkits, to easily reboot into an entirely new NAND image from storage. Has been reversed to allow shadowbooting on glitched retail consoles.

  • Retail image - A legitimate retail NAND image

  • Devkit image - A legitimate devkit NAND image

  • 1f - The original 4xxx kernel exploit rebooter images; Reboots into XeLL, Requires JTAG

  • 2f - The modern xeBuild rebooter images; Reboots into FreeBoot, Requires JTAG

  • Glitch1 - A Single-CB hacked image, Requires RGH1 or JTAG

  • Glitch2 - A Split-CB hacked image, Requires RGH2 or 1.2

  • Glitch3 - A Split-CB hacked image with a intermediate CB (CB_X). We (GGX) handle this by combining it with CB_B. Requires RGH 3 or 1.3

  • Glitch*m - A glitch image with emulated eFuses

  • Glitch*s - A glitch image with no patchslots

  • DevGl - A Devkit image that loads a retail kernel with Glitch patches

  • RGL - A 50/50 image, patches retail CD to load devkit kernel

  • JTAG - The Kernel SMC hack used by 1f and 2f images

  • RGH 1 - The original reset glitch, for single-cb.

  • RGH 2 - The newer, but worse, reset glitch for split-cb. Designed for Slims before 1.2v2 was found

  • RGH 3 - The newest, chipless glitch.

  • RGH 1.2 - Porting RGH 1 to Split-CB, v2 ports it to slims

  • RGH 1.3 - RGH 1.2v2, but using an intermediate CB for a more reliable glitch

  • xeBuild patch - 3 or 4 section Builder bootloader patch file

  • Loaderpatch - 1 section Builder patch file

  • RGLoader patch - Builder or Memory 1 section library patch file

  • CDXell patch - Memory multi section patch filesystem patch

docs.title: eMMC NANDs
docs.text: My personal research on Xbox 360 eMMC NANDs. You should read spare_data and flashfs before this one.

docs.title: Spare Data
docs.text: eMMC models (like the Corona 4GB) do not utilize Spare Data appended to the end of blocks natively. Because the physical Phison eMMC controller built into the motherboard handles bad block error detection and wear leveling entirely in hardware (exactly like a modern USB or SSD), the OS sees a perfectly contiguous, clean memory space chunked strictly by 0x200 logical boundaries.

docs.title: FlashFS (LBP Discovery)
docs.text:
Because of the lack of Spare Data, the FlashFS on eMMC diverges from the legacy 16MB/BigBlock methodologies where sequence timestamps and BlockType are tracked in 16 trailing bytes per page.

Instead of scanning page-by-page, the 4GB eMMC utilizes a Static Partition Table (the LBP - Logical Block Partitioning header).

  • Location: The LBP metadata usually begins at a reserved region near the end of the 4GB space or at a fixed offset (often sector 0x1F000 for the system update shadow partitions).
  • Structure: Instead of peeking at 0xC of the spare, the system reads a centralized table that contains:
  • Partition Name: (e.g., SystemRoot, MobileB)
  • Start Sector: The absolute sector offset.
  • Size in Sectors: The partition's total footprint.
  • Version/Sequence: Equivalent to FsSequence, used to determine the newest active partition if multiple exist.

To support eMMC natively in GGX, we will need to implement a parser for the LBP Partition Table header rather than the FsSpareData scanner used for legacy NANDs.

docs.title: NAND Blocks FlashFS
docs.text:
My personal research on the Small block / Big block NAND Flash File System.

Allot of what i know here is from RGBuild.

You should read Spare Data before reading this.

docs.title: File System
docs.text:
The FlashFS is mostly made of a dedicated sector of nand blocks with two intertwined datasets inside the even and odd numbered pages.

Inside the FlashFS root sector, the block structure changes based on page type.

docs.title: Even numbered pages
docs.text:
Even-numbered pages contain the logical chain map. They act like a classic FAT array, continuous array of uint16 big endian values.

  • Each index basically represents a physical cluster.
  • Value 0x1FFE generally means Unallocated/Free.
  • Value 0x1FFF indicates the Absolute End of a File Chain.

To read the absolute chain for a file, you find the file's starting block, read that index in the block map, and trace the pointers until it yields 0x1FFF.

docs.title: Odd-numbered pages
docs.text:
Odd-numbered pages inside the root hold the directory listings, known as FileSystemEntry in RGBuild.

Each entry uses 32 bytes (0x20), structured sequentially without complex nesting:

Offset Length Type Description 0x00 0x16 Ascii String The filename, padded with \0. If the first byte is 0x05, the file is marked deleted. 0x16 0x02 UInt16 (BE) The absolute starting block number linked into the Block Map. 0x18 0x04 UInt32 (BE) The exact payload size inside the block chain. 0x1C 0x04 Int32 (BE) Standard DOS-formatted timestamp.

docs.title: Creating Files
docs.text:
When creating or modifying an entry, you:

  1. Find an adjacent sequence of 0x1FFE allocations in the Block Map of adequate size.
  2. Link them together by replacing 0x1FFE with the physical pointers.
  3. Overwrite the odd-paged metadata registry with the new 32-byte directory string referencing the starting block you allocated.

docs.title: ExposureMG's Xbox 360 Research
docs.text: The concepts i've learned whilst making GGX that i dont think are documented enough.

docs.title: Index
docs.text:
• Spare Data Small block, Big Block, Big On Small

  • FlashFS XSB and PSB/KSB, SB and BB

  • eMMC NANDs eMMC Metadata and FlashFS

  • Patches xeBuild, RGLP and Loaderpatch

  • STFS PIRS System Update packages

docs.title: NAND Patches
docs.text: My personal research on xeBuild Patches (BIN), RGLoader Patches (RGLP) and LoaderPatch Patches (TXT)

docs.title: xeBuild Patches
docs.text: The binaries are made built from assembly patch files. A full repository of xeBuild patch sources with a build script can be found on Mitchell Waite's GitHub.

docs.title: Binaries
docs.text:
xeBuild patchsets (XEPATCH0 header?) are made up of 3 or 4 different patch sections, seperated by FFFFFF.

Modern JTAG - 1BL - CB - CD - Kernel / Hypervisor

Reset Glitch (GG) - CB / CB_B - CD - Kernel / Hypervisor

Individual patches have no header data and are just a compiled asm bin.

docs.title: Patch Sections
docs.text:
Invidual sections are treated differently; CB/CB_B/CD are read and applied directly by xeBuild, whereas Kernel / Hypervisor patches are applied by the patched CD on boot.

xeBuild patch sections are structured as followed:

Offset Type Description 0x0 Unsigned 32-bit integer Target Address 0x4 Unsigned 32-bit integer Target Amount 0x8 Unsigned 32-bit integer array Patch Data From: Emma's Research

docs.title: CD / KHV Patches
docs.text:
Also Read: CD Patches and Kernel Patches

Also See: Raw xeBuild CD/4BL and KVH patches

CD patches work differently, not only do they disable panics and emulate fuses, they contain a patching engine that loads and applies Kernel/Hypervisor patches in memory. This way, the base 1888 kernel and hypervisor can remain untouched, and all modifications happen in memory.

docs.title: General use-cases
docs.text:
• CB / CB_B -> Enable post codes / uart, virtual fuses, break trust chain for reset glitch
  • CD -> Load and apply kernel / hypervisor patches
  • Kernel / Hypervisor -> Unsigned code execution, debugging, the actual OS modifications

docs.title: DashLaunch Patches
docs.text: DashLaunch is the companion in-hypervisor runtime patcher for xeBuild. Requires CD to have already patched Kernel / Hypervisor for unsigned code execution. Reads launch.ini from the flashfs root, and applies listed plugins / patches.

docs.title: Loaderpatch Patches
docs.text:
Loaderpatch (By stoker25) is the bootloader patcher for RGBuild. As simple as it gets, just asm stored in a text file, uncompiled.

I plan to add support for Loaderpatch to gxBuild, most likely through an external app that converts them to xebuild binaries.

Loaderpatch Sources: RGLoader-Patches

docs.title: RGLoader Patches
docs.text:
RGLP Sources: RGLoader-Patches

RGBuild has a custom patching format for their Kernel / XAM / Hypervisor patches: RGLP. I believe the latest version of xeBuild supports these patches.

This format can be applied directly to a loader or applied in-memory by RGLoader.xex.

TODO: RGLoader Patch Format

docs.title: Meta and Spare Data
docs.text:
My personal research on the Xbox 360 NAND Spare data, metadata and headers.

Here i will go into block types and spare data layout.

docs.title: Block Types
docs.text:
There are 2 main block types:

(Bytes) Big Block Small Block Page Data Size 2048 512 Spare Size 64 16 Total Page Size 2112 528 Total Pages 64 32 Total Blocks 4096 1024 Block Size 16,896 135,168

On big block NANDs, for compatibility, the southbridge splits each page into 4 small block sub pages. The NAND dumper (Probably J-Runner with Extras) should've already split the page and interleaved the spare data.

For 16mb images running an image with a larger FlashFS, such as 17489 devkit conversions, big blocks can be used alongside a filesystems folder on the HDD.

There is also eMMC (4GB) which has no spare data.

docs.title: Small Block Layout
docs.text:
The 16 end bytes (Spare Data) contain metadata for remapping, FlashFS, and bad block detection.

Layout Motherboards Block ID Offset Bad Block Marker XSB Xenon, Zephyr, Falcon spare[0x0..0x2] spare[0x5] PSB/KSB Jasper, Trinity, Corona spare[0x1..0x3] spare[0x5]

Key Differences: - XSB (Xenon Southbridge): The Logical Block ID is placed at the absolute start of the spare area. - PSB/KSB (Panda & Koala Southbridges): A leading zero byte (0x00) is prepended to the Block ID, shifting it by one byte. This was likely introduced to standardize the spare layout across larger page sizes later.

docs.title: Big Block Layout
docs.text:
Big Block NANDs (256/512MB) utilize a 2048+64 byte physical page structure. While physically larger, the console controller still interacts with the data in 512-byte logical sub-pages.

Layout Block ID Offset Bad Block Marker Big Block spare[0x1..0x3] spare[0x0]

docs.title: Spare Data Layout
docs.text:
While Big Block and Small Block physically append different sizing formats, the overarching protocol uses a 16-byte (0x10) metadata frame attached to each logical 512-byte (0x200) page chunk.

Within the 16 bytes, the Xbox 360 stores important info for file reconstruction:

  • Block ID (UInt16 LE): Identifies the original physical target.
  • FsSequence (UInt32 BE): An incrementing timestamp order indicating the newest/latest rewrite iteration of a cluster.
  • Bad Block Indicator (UInt8): Sits specifically at index 5 (SmallBlock) or index 0 (BigBlock). 0xFF states the block is clean.
  • FsSize (UInt16 BE)
  • FsPageCount (UInt8)
  • FsBlockType (UInt8): Bits holding what type of data the block contains (like 0x30 or 0x2C for FlashFS Root metadata). Usually piggybacked atop the ECC.

docs.title: Bad Blocks
docs.text:
Unlike normal solid-state setups, the Xbox 360 does not use a FAT-like table or map for tracking bad pages, instead, bad blocks are managed physically:

  • During hardware checks, if a block is deemed corrupt, a flag is dropped directly into its unadulterated spare data frame. For example, in Small Block layouts, if byte 0x05 of the 16-byte spare chunk is 0x00 instead of 0xFF, the block is bad.

  • The replacement block is taken from the hardware reserve sector (the final 32 blocks of your logical partition, typically offset 0x3E0 for 16MB or 0x1E0 for Big Blocks). The physical block ID of the bad block is written into the spare data of the reserve replacement block, establishing an implicit absolute link.

docs.title: STFS
docs.text: My research on STFS, Specifically PIRS for system updates.

docs.title: gxBuild Developer
docs.text:
the gxBuild session by nature is designed to be used as a NAND editing API. It can easily be interacted with in other rust projects via cargo.

gxBuild also exposes the session to FFI if enabled. This allows you to euse gxBuild with many other languages.

docs.title: Session
docs.text:
The session is the core wrapper for gxBuild. Whilst builder exposes the required functions and structs, the session turns these into human readable high level commands.

The session uses a queue/commands system, where all build commands (except those run with RunOnce) are added to a queue and executed in priority order. This by design is to prevent repeat operations, allow huge batch nand editing, and give an easy API for a CLI, TUI, GUI and Shell.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: There are 2 categories of commands, Session commands and Builder commands.

docs.title: Session Commands
docs.text:
SessionInit { base: Option<PathBuf>, common: Option<PathBuf> },
SessionList,
SessionDelete { id: u8 },
SessionRun,

ParseIni { content: String, filename: String, target: String, ini_base: PathBuf, common: PathBuf },
ParseImage { path: PathBuf, key: Option<[u8; 16]> },
ParseKey { key: [u8; 16] },
ParseKeybin { key: Option<[u8; 16]> },
ParseFlashfs { path: PathBuf },
ParsePatch { path: PathBuf },
ApplyPatch { path: PathBuf, ptype: u8, target: Option<u8> },
Extract { id: String },
ExtractAll,
Replace { id: u8, path: PathBuf },
List,
Delete { id: u8 },
Clear,
Compress,
Decompress,
Update { path: PathBuf },
Build { output: PathBuf, target: u8 },
FinalizeFlashfs,

#[cfg(feature = "python")]
PythonShell,
#[cfg(feature = "python")]
RunPythonScript { path: PathBuf },
CreateImage { layout: crate::core::data::blocks::NandLayout },
ExtractStfs { path: PathBuf, target_dir: PathBuf },

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: gxBuild is a rust xbox 360 image patcher and builder.

docs.title: Patcher
docs.text: gxBuild xeBuild RGLP Loaderpatch FreeMyXe XeUnshackle Bootloaders ✅ ✅ ❌ ✅ ❌ ❌ SMC ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ ❌ ❌ KHV ✅ ❌ ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ In-Memory ✅ ✅ ✅ ❌ ✅ ✅ Sections 5 3 / 4 1 1 N/A N/A

docs.title: Parser and Builder
docs.text: gxBuild xeBuild RGBuild Retail ✅ ✅ ✅ Devkit ✅ ✅ ✅ 1f ✅ ❌ ❌ 2f ✅ ✅ ✅ 2f-s ✅ ❌ ❌ glitch1 ✅ ✅ ✅ glitch2 ✅ ✅ ✅ glitch3 ✅ ❌ ❌ glitch2m ✅ ✅ ❌ Block Types All All Big Southbridge ✅ ✅ ✅

docs.title: gxBuild scripting
docs.text: gxBuild includes a rust-python interpreter and exports all data / functions into it upon being called. This allows for hyper specialized build configurations and easy tests without writing concrete rust code.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: There are two ways to invoke the interpreter: - gxbuild <build/info> -n <script> - Run a script - gxbuild shell - Spawn a full shell

docs.title: Script
docs.text: Regular python script; gxBuild session exported as gxsession python module, core builder functions available as gxbuild. All CLI inputted information is available through the gxsession module. Any cli nand edits will be executed after the script.

docs.title: Shell
docs.text: As seen in the title; A python shell spawned mid-session allowing interactive edits on the fly. run session commands with the gxsession handler.

docs.title: gxBuild Usage
docs.text:
The utility can be used for a range of purposes; Simple metadata extraction, full patchset application, devkit NAND building all the way to shadowboot and xell creation.

The CLI has 4 main methods: Build (default), info, shell, tui. A quick rundown: - Build: Parser and Build a nand from a dump or parts - Info: Quickly extract metadata, with or without key - Shell: Launch python shell - TUI: TUI NAND editor

docs.title: Build
docs.text:
The main mode of gxBuild. Inputs a build folder and type, console type, and data files, and builds a new nand according to the spec of the chosen build type.

Default outputs a NAND image, can also output a shadowboot or xell image.

docs.title: Info
docs.text: Version for with and without cpu key. Extract as much metadata as possible and report back to user.

docs.title: Shell
docs.text: See scripting.md. Python shell with CLI data inputted.

docs.title: TUI
docs.text: Archinstall-inspired NAND image generator.

docs.title: Arguements

docs.title: Xbox 360 Reversing
docs.text:
An overview about xeBuild’s hacked images

By DrSchottky - 10-10-2015 Original Page (Internet Archive): RaizelConsole

Dumped and reformatted by ExposureMG - 03-27-2026

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: All the stuff i'll write comes from what i read and/or reversed. It might be inaccurate or totally wrong, please don't blame me for this.

docs.title: Intro
docs.text:
As you might know, Xbox’s chain of trust is composed by a series of loaders and ends with the Kernel (I won’t dwell on this, boot process is explained quiet well on Free60), but how is made a modified bootchain? Assuming that you know what an ECC is and how it works, let’s focus on xeBuild’s patches.

To avoid confusion: all my examples will be based on RGH2 Trinity, with MFG CB_A(9188) !

There are three items that need to be patched on a hacked image: CB_B, CD and Kernel/HV; and this is what xeBuild does.

CB_B/CD patches are applied to the original files before they’re used to build an image, Kernel patches are stored as plaintext in the built image and applied at runtime by CD on an already decompressed and merged CE + CG (for this reason CF doesn’t need to be patched).

xeBuild’s patches format is documented in about_patches.S, and in summary is:

[CB_B patches] FF FF FF FF [CD patches] FF FF FF FF [Kernel patches] FF FF FF FF

Each patch looks like this:

  • Address (32 bit)
  • Number of patches (32 bit)
  • Data (32 bit x number of patches)

If you want to look inside a loader (CB_A/B, CD, CF)

  • load it into IDA;
  • set ppc as processor;
  • look at 0x8 (4 bytes) for the entry point and jump to it;
  • press C to disassemble.

Loading into IDA a kernel image requires some further step since it’s stored in your nand as a compressed base (CE) + update (CG).

I think that RGLoader Image Editor is the quickest way to get an updated kernel (ready to be disassembled) from your nand dump.

Most of Crypto/Hash functions used in loaders are equal to those used in the kernel: if you have a kernel with symbols it’s easy to make a labeler script that searches for well known patterns in your code.

In later chapters we’ll see in detail the various patches applied by xeBuild.

>> Next Chapter >>

Page 3 is missing, if you have it, please send it through. Every other page has been crawled on the Wayback Machine.

  • wurthless-electroniks' modern_loadfare Universal CB Patcher

docs.title: Chapter 1: CB_B patches
docs.text: By DrSchottky - 10-10-2015 Original Page (Internet Archive): RaizelConsole

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: All the stuff i'll write comes from what i read and/or reversed. It might be inaccurate or totally wrong, please don't blame me for this.

docs.title: Intro
docs.text:
Before you start reading: executables and patches are written in Assembly (O RLY?). If you don’t know what ASM is and/or hardware basics you can stop your reading here, sorry.

Let’s take our disassembled CB_B 9188 (we’re working on a Trinity, do you remember?) and start looking the related patch file. Oh, as you probably noticed all instructions have fixed length. This is very helpful!

docs.title: Patch #1
docs.text:
00 00 4D F4 00 00 00 01 60 00 00 00

That’s pretty easy! It replaces whatever’s at 0x4DF4 with 0x60000000 (opcode for nop). But what’s at 0x4DF4? CB LDV (fuseline 02). The first check compares the position (counting from left) of the rightmost F in fuseline 02 with data stored at 0x3B1 (sequence byte), and if it doesn’t match it jumps to the second check (otherwise it’ll continue boot). We patch that jump with a nop.

docs.title: Patch #2
docs.text:
00 00 4F 50 00 00 00 03 60 00 00 00 60 00 00 00 60 00 00 00

Three nops starting from 0x4F50, cut off error 0xA3 (haven’t found info about it nor tried to reverse what is).

docs.title: Patch #3
docs.text:
00 00 56 60 00 00 00 01 38 60 00 00

Replaces call to XeCryptMemDiff (that checks CD hash) with li r3,0. This because next instruction checks for r3 (which should be XeCryptMemDiff’s return value) comparing it with 0: If they’re equal boot continues.

>> Next Chapter >>

docs.title: Credits
docs.text:
• xeBuild Team
  • Free60
  • RGLoader

docs.title: Chapter 2: CD patches
docs.text: By DrSchottky - 10-10-2015 Original Page (Internet Archive): RaizelConsole

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: All the stuff i'll write comes from what i read and/or reversed. It might be inaccurate or totally wrong, please don't blame me for this.

docs.title: Patch #1
docs.text:
00 00 02 8C 00 00 00 01 48 00 4C 95

Jumps to a custom subroutine located at 0x4F20 (Patch #4).

docs.title: Patch #2
docs.text:
00 00 05 B4 00 00 00 01 48 00 4C 38 00

Jumps to a custom subroutine located at 0x51EC.

docs.title: Patch #3
docs.text:
00 00 08 30 00 00 00 01 60 00 00 00

nop a check during CF execution.

docs.title: Patch #4
docs.text:
00 00 4F 20 00 00 00 DC ...

Custom subroutine, basically it's GliGli's CD with extra stuff. It asks SMC how the console was turned on and starts kernel or xell.

At 0x509C, 0x50BC, 0x51C0 and 0x51DC there are other subcalls used by 0x4F20. They init PCI, regs, SMC and other stuff.

Subroutine at 0x51EC (called by Patch #2) loads from Flash Kernel/HV patches, apllies them and jumps to HV.

>> Next Chapter >>

docs.title: Chapter 4: Kernel Patches
docs.text: By DrSchottky - 10-10-2015 Original Page (Internet Archive): RaizelConsole

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: All the stuff i'll write comes from what i read and/or reversed. It might be inaccurate or totally wrong, please don't blame me for this.

docs.title: Intro
docs.text:
Ok, things start to get interesting. I originally planned to write a single thread for HV + Kernel pacthes, but i realized that there would be too many things to write, making the thread chaotic.

In this chapter i'll try to understand and explain Kernel pacthes that, in brief, remove XEXs' security measures and (some) DVD drive checks.

How can we split HV and Kernel? Take your CE+CG (decompressed, decrypted etc etc):

  • HV: begin - 0x3FFFF (the first 256KB)
  • Kernel: 0x40000 - end

Patches mainly spoofs value returned by functions, so i'll not go into details.

This is the patchset for Kernel 16574

docs.title: Patch #1
docs.text:
00 07 B1 50 00 00 00 02 38 60 00 00 4E 80 00 20

Forces XexpConvertError to return 0, whatever r3's value is.

docs.title: Patch #2
docs.text:
00 07 BC E8 00 00 00 01 38 60 00 01

Replace XexpVerifyMediaType call with li r3, 1, allowing XEX booting from any media.

docs.title: Patch #3
docs.text:
00 07 BD F8 00 00 00 01 38 60 00 00

Replace RtlImageXexHeaderString call with li r3, 0.

docs.title: Patch #6
docs.text:
00 07 A7 38 00 00 00 02 38 60 00 00 4E 80 00 20

XexpVerifyMinimumVersion returns immediatly 0.

docs.title: Patch #7
docs.text:
00 09 44 28 00 00 00 01 3A E0 00 10

Modifies the value of a register in SfcxInspectLargeDataBlock. Maybe something related to flash layout(dunno).

docs.title: Patch #8
docs.text:
00 09 8D 80 00 00 00 01 2B 0B 00 FF

In SataCdRomAuthenticationExInitialize compares r11 with FF instead of 1, altering branch path.

docs.title: Patch #9
docs.text:
00 09 87 64 00 00 00 05 38 60 00 00 60 00 00 00 60 00 00 00 60 00 00 00 60 00 00 00

Removes E66 from SataCdRomActivateHCDFRuntimePatch.

docs.title: Patches #10,#11...,#20
docs.text:
They all do the same thing: after stack frame init they set r3 (0 or 1) and return. Target functions are:

  • XeKeysVerifyRSASignature
  • XeKeysSecurityConvertError
  • XeKeysDvdAuthExConvertError
  • _XeKeysRevokeIsValid
  • XeKeysRevokeIsRevoked
  • _XeKeysRevokeIsRevoked
  • XeKeysRevokeIsDeviceRevoked
  • XeKeysRevokeConvertError
  • XeKeysConsoleSignatureVerification (for this one the patch is a little different, but nevermind)
  • XeCryptBnQwBeSigVerify
  • SataDiskAuthenticateDevice

docs.title: Patch #21
docs.text: It's a payload placed into the almost "dead" body of SataDiskAuthenticateDevice. This code will be called by subsequent patches.

docs.title: Patch #22
docs.text:
00 06 13 D0 00 00 00 01 48 0F 8F 01

After XAM init branches to the first chunk of the payload which loads launch.xex.

docs.title: Patch #23
docs.text:
00 07 CF 68 00 00 00 01 48 0D D3 A5

XexLoadExecutable is patched to bl to the second chunk of the payload. TBH i don't know what kind of black magic it does.

docs.title: Patch #24
docs.text:
00 10 9C 78 00 00 00 01 48 05 06 BC

XeKeysGetKeyProperties is a wrapper for HvxKeysGetKeyProperties. It branches to the third chunk of our payload (instead of HvxKeysGetKeyProperties) and, if function's argument is equal to 0x14 it does the same black magic of Patch #23 (keeps reading data at 0x12345678 until it's 0, dunno why).

>> Next Chapter >>

docs.title: Chapter 5: Miscellaneous kernel patches
docs.text: By DrSchottky - 10-10-2015 Original Page (Internet Archive): RaizelConsole

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: All the stuff i'll write comes from what i read and/or reversed. It might be inaccurate or totally wrong, please don't blame me for this.

docs.title: Intro
docs.text: Now let's take a look at the various kernel patches provided with xeBuild. These are disabled by default, but can be enabled under particular circumstances.

docs.title: nofcrt.bin
docs.text:
Removes ODD C/R requirement

00 06 11 A0 00 00 00 01 60 00 00 00

Nop the branch to XeKeysFcrtLoad, during post 0x6C (INIT_SECURITY)

00 02 D7 E0 00 00 00 03 2B 0A 00 0F 60 00 00 00 3B E0 00 00

HV patch, not reversed yet.

docs.title: nohdd.bin
docs.text:
Disable HDD

00 15 A6 20 00 00 00 01 38 60 00 00

Replace branch to SataChannelDetectDevice (subcall of SataDiskInitialize) with li r3,0 (faking return value)

docs.title: nohdmiwait.bin
docs.text:
Disable HDCP handshake

00 15 85 90 00 00 00 01 48 00 00 4C

In HalpReadCurrentAVPack exclude bl HalpRebootSystem from branch case (turns bne into b).

docs.title: nointmu.bin
docs.text:
Disables internal MU

00 08 D6 A8 00 00 00 01 3D 40 60 00

Spoofs mobo revision(?) in SfcxMuMountStorage

00 0E 35 20 00 00 00 01 3D 40 60 00

Spoofs mobo revision(?) in MassDriverEntry

00 08 E9 D0 00 00 00 01 4E 80 00 20

MmcxMuMountStorage returns immediatly to caller.

docs.title: nolan.bin
docs.text:
Disable LAN PHY

00 0D 2D DC 00 00 00 01 60 00 00 00

Nop bl _NicInit_CNicEmac__QAAXXZ from NicDriverEntry (called during driver init)

>> Next Chapter >>

docs.title: Chapter 6: Find and patch Kernel Errors
docs.text: By DrSchottky - 10-10-2015 Original Page (Internet Archive): RaizelConsole

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text:
All the stuff i'll write comes from what i read and/or reversed. It might be inaccurate or totally wrong, please don't blame me for this.

Black screen with white text saying to contact customer service, followed by a Exx. Those are kernel errors, and some of them (mainly DVD, HDD and ETH errors) can be bypassed with kernel patches (obviously you bypass the error, the device you excluded from kernel won't work).

How can we find those checks in kernel?

VdDisplayFatalError is a function that takes the error as parameter (r3, as always) and shows it on Video/ROL.

Once you have your kernel (and symbols) you can find a pattern that uniquely identifies the function and search for it in your CE+CG image (IDC scripting is the way!).

Once you found the function in your kernel you simply look for callers. The last byte of r3 before the bl is the hex of the error that will be shown.

An example of a error that COULD be easily bypassed? E64. An example of a error that COULD NOT be easily bypassed? E74.

Here's a little gift

#include <idc.idc>

static main()
{
    auto addr = 0;
    addr = FindBinary(addr,SEARCH_DOWN,"DB E1 FF D0 94 21 FE D0");
    MakeNameEx(addr - 8, "VdDisplayFatalError", 0);
}

>> Next Chapter >>

docs.title: Chapter 7: Setting up Syscall table
docs.text: By DrSchottky - 10-10-2015 Original Page (Internet Archive): RaizelConsole

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text:
All the stuff i'll write comes from what i read and/or reversed. It might be inaccurate or totally wrong, please don't blame me for this.

Syscalls are the mechanism used by unprivileged code (like Kernel) to request functionalities to privileged code (HyperVisor).

HV is the most privileged code running on your system: it manages memory access, encryption and low-level security.

Syscalls are invoked by unprivileged code writing in r0 the number of the required Syscall (from 0x00 to 0xDEPENDS_ON_KERNEL) and executing sc instruction.

sc throws an exception that is catched and moves execution to 0xc00. At that address there's the syscall dispatcher that, after checking r0 validity, looks in sycall table for the effective address of the required syscall and jump to it.

Syscall table start address is a dword located at 0x48 (HV header) so, to get the address of a syscall implementation you have too look at sctable_start_address+(syscall number * 4).

Syscall table is a simple list of sequential 32 bit addresses, ordered by syscall number (ascending).

This is the sctable for HV 12625. As you can see syscall #0 is at 0x1F20, syscall #1 at 0x8B4 etc etc..

MISSING: syscall.png

In newer Kernels syscall addressing changes a bit: for some syscalls the address in the table doesn't lead to syscall implementation, but to a code snippet then dinamically create the "real" address.

>> Outro >>
